summary: Dan Shapiro shares a five-minute talk he gave at ORD Camp, an unconference, addressed to fellow white men about recognizing their default behaviors around speaking and taking up space. He argues that white men are socialized to speak and teach even when they're not the most knowledgeable, and that this crowds out other voices. He offers three practical tools: plan which sessions to lead vs. listen in, take notes to resist the urge to speak, and mentally roll a d20 before talking (only speak on a natural 20). He illustrates the problem with an anecdote about white men dominating a session on underrepresentation in tech, and frames the advice as making the event better for everyone.
key_points: White men benefit from socialized comfort in speaking up, which can inadvertently crowd out other voices in group settings
The practical d20 framework: in a room of 20 people, mentally roll a 20-sided die and only speak on a natural 20, giving yourself roughly a 5% speaking share
Self-awareness requires concrete tools, not just good intentions — planning ahead, taking notes, and physical reminders help override default behavior
At a previous ORD Camp session on underrepresentation in tech, most words were spoken by white men despite most attendees being underrepresented people
money_quotes: Talking about race and gender is deeply uncomfortable for me. It's probably uncomfortable for you too. But that's OK – we're here to grow, and to learn, and we'll stumble through it together.
A common white dude default, certainly my default, is speaking/teaching – even when, perhaps, we're not the world's greatest authority on the topic.
Every time something interesting happens, roll a d20 in your head, and speak up on a natural 20.
Jot down what's being said – you'll get more from the session, and, crucially, it makes it easier to keep your mouth shut.
At ORD camp two years ago I was in a session on underrepresentation in tech. Most of the attendees were underrepresented people in tech. Most of the words were spoken by white dudes.

Being a white dude means we have power and privilege. And there's a set of default behaviors associated with power and privilege that can be pretty toxic to what we all want to accomplish here together.
key_insight: Good intentions about making space for others require concrete behavioral tools — like the d20 rule — because socialized defaults are too strong to override with awareness alone.
